sentences.text: Early.
I don't know the hour but-- but very early.
And you got-- did we get something?
I think some black coffee or some-- some black water.
They called it coffee.
Some black water, I think, like that.
The bread we had from the ration what we got in the evening.
In the evening, it was a loaf of bread about 10 inches long cut in five shares and everyone get one piece like that.
And that has to be for 24 hours.
And on top of that, we had then in the evening some kind of a soup.
A soup-- was it the chaff from wheat?
They made a soup.
We called it a spit soup because we couldn't eat it.
They had more to spit out than to eat.
No one, they took it to work.
Did you have an [? appeal ?] first?
Yeah.
There was an [? appeal ?] and the work in the-- in-- there has to be always-- the [INAUDIBLE] people.
It always has to be-- has to be, if someone was missing, there was no [? appeal. ?] So sometimes somebody didn't stay right or was not ready right or whatever.
No, it was something, you got 25 with the whip.
And it was never smooth.
There was always some kind, some-- some excuse to-- to-- to be not nice, to hit or-- it never went smooth, let's just-- I don't remember the details, but it was never smooth enough.
And then we worked.
They sent everybody in a group went to their work.
Some people worked at sorting clothes.
That's most of the work, most of it.
And some went-- there was group did shoemakers, [? clear ?] tailors, goldsmith.
They had-- there were-- they had barracks in the same where we slept, in the same barracks.
In the area they had places.
So they went to their work there.
And there was a wald commando which worked in the wald, in the woods, wald commandos, woods.
And-- and later in the evening when we went back, as always, they kept us running and-- and running and singing.
And it was always something.
We never had smooth that we went in going and coming.
There was some punishment always.
Whatever it is, there was some punishment.
They'd have to run and fell down on the ground and run again for hours, some [? multiple ?] tasks for hours.
Push-up-- not push-up.
Running, fell down, and up on the ground.
Not push-up.
Push-up is pushing up.
Now there's-- that was always something.
We had experience, a lot of it.
Almost every day was something.
Da, somebody took-- when we were sorting the clothes, most people brought with them cans of food and valuables and things like that.
And then all these things went through the hand from the people with the sorting.
So if somebody took us some can of food and they opened, they was caught by the Germans, he was shot.
And everybody has to see how it is.
They put them on a carrier and they bring him around.
Everybody can see.
That happened if you opened a can of sardines or whatever.
But regardless of the people with work, they-- they took the courage to steal your food.
And matter of fact, some of us was very good and it was risky.
But she did it as a woman.
She could more hide in, things like that.
And they were not so quick with a woman.
They more suspected a man will do these things.
So we had fairly sometimes we have transport where we had some food special that what kept us [INAUDIBLE] alive, really.
So-- but the punishment was very severe.
There's no any-- any-- any excuses.
If they caught you, dead.
No problem.
None.
Did you do anything that would have gotten you into trouble?
To be very honest, I don't remember that I was stealing food.
I tried to-- to avoid them, as I say.
I tried-- I don't remember that I stole food.
Maybe I did, maybe not.
I don't know.
I don't remember this.
So-- but we had-- we had-- the angst was so-- to be afraid was so big that you really, if you-- you knew you want-- maybe I [? come ?] [? it ?] over.
You were thinking, let me try to avoid these things like that.
So--
Separating the clothes and then bring it to the-- all the papers and-- papers, old papers [? stuck ?] with everything, it went to the fireplace.
So there was a special place where they burned all these kind of things.
And I had a friend what did this kind of work.
He burned papers and things.
So every time when I brought some stuff there, I sometimes brought with them valuable things.
As I say, in the luggage from the people.
Yeah.
Well, I-- whatever I could do to sabotage, we did it.
But you have to be careful.
For a can of food, I didn't think it was so worthwhile to do it.
But that, I figure it's less danger in it because they-- you didn't expect they would check it when you go with this-- with this stuff.
So I was hiding there.
Give it to him, for him, too, and for myself.
And I hide it because I was thinking once maybe one day we might go away from here.
And then when we do, we need some valuables with us.
What kinds of valuables were they?
It was dollars, diamonds, gold rings.
Although it was very strict there because we had everything to give up there.
Not that the Germans didn't steal by themselves.
They-- they stole because when they went every three months on vacation, the [INAUDIBLE] they took with them the nicest things they could find.
There were suitcases so big you can hardly carry it.
And valuables, really, valuables what were supposed to go to the German government.
They had control of it.
So they took with them over it sometimes.
Some people like kapos sometimes have access to the Germans and they talk with them.
So they told them, save me.
They talked to me that.
So they-- they stole on their own.
But not us.
We sure were not allowed to do that.
Well, I-- I don't know the name of it.
I don't know the name, but volk-- people which were later, which there were more.
And the camp-- the head of the camp I didn't see so often because they didn't come out so quick.
The [NON-ENGLISH], there was a Wagner, there was a Frenzel, [NON-ENGLISH] Wolf, two brothers Wolf, a Bauer, and a few more.
They were Germans.
The guards.
And then we had about 150 Ukrainian guards.
But with them we had little contact.
They were just guards for-- excuse me-- for the post rounds, the tower, the--
Towers.
And they were also-- but we didn't have really any direct contact with them, really.
So they didn't have the authority unless they were told by Germans, do something.
Otherwise, they didn't beat us or they didn't-- because they didn't have this authority to do that.
The kapos-- the Jewish kapos-- there was only Jewish kapos.
No.
They-- what they were only is before an [? appeal, ?] they brought the group together to prepare the [? appeal. ?] And by their work, they were there.
But they didn't had any authority to-- Well, a matter of fact, we had one German Jew who was a kapo because he spoke so good German and he was from Germany.
So he thought that he had a special privilege because of that.
So he tried to appease these Germans.
So he had some ideas.
One time he said, we should have numbers.
We didn't have any numbers.
We should have numbers.
But they-- because there was a small group, this never had-- never realized in our camp there.
And also, whatever he saw, we talk among ourselves, he went and tell the Germans.
So was really a traitor.
So one day, he went with this-- with this stories what he told.
He didn't tell to Frenzel what was the second thing in command.
And he went to the-- the-- the Jewish German went to the first one.
So this Frenzel was mad at him that he doesn't come to him.
So-- so we-- we-- we went to this Frenzel, the kapos went to the Frenzel and they told him about the German Jew that he does something, whatever.
So the German said, you know down there what you do with somebody what-- no.
What's the right word for it?
A traitor?
Traitor.
He said, you put a blanket over his head and that's what you do, what we did.
Told.
So that's what we did.
And he wants to get rid of him too, because he found-- he should come to me to tell me then.
He wanted the [INAUDIBLE] he was touched about that thing.
So--
No.
I mean, the Jewish-- I was not directly involved then.
Yeah, I knew it would happen because they-- we had a magazine where-- of a warehouse where it was utensils, medicine, and things like that.
And it was-- one friend of mine, he was the head of the medical department.
In other words, if it was just to give a pill, he was good there.
So-- so he gave him a potion of poison.
And they-- and they gave him that and they killed him in the barrack.
So I was not there.
I was not involved in it.
I was not a big hero in that [INAUDIBLE] sort of thing.
I-- I tried-- in an easy way, maybe I can come through that.
But I changed when I-- later on when I would tell you about the uprising-- I don't probably-- someone probably told you that already.
So--
All right.
That was-- there is some transport that came with some Jewish people which have some instrument.
Now the Germans were always to-- tries to-- to hurt us and to-- and also some satisfaction they get out of it.
They made us-- while here was a big fire burning, they were satisfied of-- of a joy for them to make us Jews, the people who are working there, to dance there.
So they made this instrument-- the people played this instrument--
Musical instruments.
And they had to play and we had to dance.
Can you imagine?
A fire was burning here, our relatives, the family we knew, went to fire, and we had to dance.
So, and I asked Selma to dance, and that is what we met.
So we always-- since we always were almost as much we could be together.
So a matter of fact, one of the German called us bride and groom.
So Frenzel, matter of fact, was it.
But anyway, so we-- whenever we could be together we were together.
So what--
Not really.

You have-- you got people in the evening-- you see, when we were finished after we had eaten and all the punishments we had, we could for an hour or two we still were in the same compound around different barracks, the compound around, be together.
There was locked up and we could be together.
So we met each other.
We sat outside and talked and things like that.
So a lot of people had girlfriends.
They sat and talked and things like that.
So-- so there was some relations as far as that's concerned.
That's all.
What other kinds of things went on?
Did-- were there religious Jews who tried to--
There were some.
There were-- one of the main organizer of the uprising, there was some of a rabbi-- Friedlander.
And he-- he was a religious observing Jew.
And he, in his way, he practiced whatever he could.
So, but most of them were not observant.
So I don't remember who was observant.
But I don't think there were many.
I just heard him and maybe another one or thing.
So, but that was just did by himself, not organized, and not by-- you have-- you got an allowance of time for it.
Nothing.
Such a thing didn't happen.
It didn't-- didn't exist.
How often did the transports come?
Well, there was a time they came-- they came about three, four times a week.
And sometimes less.
The Dutch transport that came, I think, every Friday, I think, or Thursday.
Once a week.
There was once, twice.
And there was bigger transport and smaller transport.
And the first one came in in train wagons, regular passenger wagons.
Later it was just cattle wagons, though, like all of them.
Cattle wagons.
Yeah, I watched the train always.
As I said, they picked us.
It was a special wald command, wald commando, a wald.
Wald is German.
Wald is the woods.
They call it wald commando in German.
And they were, especially when a transport came, they took care of the people, go out and things like that.
But if it was not enough, they took everybody.
So I was sometimes there to help them with their luggage and people because they can't transport people, dead people, half dead, things like that.
So we had to clear it up and everything like that.
So I was sometimes at the transport there, too.
But it was not my regular work there.
You become a machine on automatic.
You do-- you do things.
The pressure is-- and you stay and watch.
They're whipping you or with the gun.
And you have to do the work.
So that is really explain, how could you do it?
But it is not-- not so to explain.
Whoever didn't experience you cannot make understand somebody how you can do it.
But you can.
But people are in extreme circumstances like that, and we knew where the people go, and what happened with them.
And-- and we did it because we were just automatic.
We didn't have to think hours ahead, the minute.
No, is it that?
You do that.
No, it's that.
You do that.
Automatically.
That's it.
It's hard to explain things.
You couldn't-- you couldn't explain somebody.
People can criticize and say, how could you do that?
You cannot judge if you're not by yourself in your same shoes.
You cannot judge-- judge it because surely my heart cried.
But I-- I wasn't thinking.
I wasn't a thinking element anymore.
I was just on automatic.
What do you think helped you to survive there?
How what?
How few?
Well, I have to come to the uprising.
We-- we always-- I always, no matter how-- how really reality it was, I always was thinking one day I will be out.
Really, I had a not realistic idea.
But somehow, I was thinking we will come out of here.
And-- and that it was the idea went around different plans.
Everybody has a lot of plans but not really realistic plans.
Till a transport came with Russian Jewish prisoners of war from Minsk.
And I say we had ideas, different ones.
And when they came, and there was among them was one captain in the Russian army.
And when he had--
Sasha Pechersky.
Pechersky, I think.
Yeah, Pechersky.
And when he came, he said-- but we told him what's going on here.
So he wanted to run away the first day.
So we told him, listen, that it's not so easy as you think.
There is guards all over and mines around, things like that.
So we told him we have plans on our own.
And-- not me.
I was not involved in it.
Let me put it this way, I don't want to make a big hero as I am.
In the group, in the Jewish, the one-- you see, I was Polish, and all my friends were involved in it.
Because I went with a Dutch girl and people what didn't speak Yiddish or Polish, they were considered be careful.
Don't talk to them because they might bubble and tell what's going on.
So that was a big secret.
So a small group only.
And mostly was it Polish Jews what they planned and they talk with Sasha, the group of his group.
And the plan they told him, as I say, you cannot run away just like that.
Has to be organized.
And he organized a plan how to run away.
It was planned for everybody.
It wasn't a plan just a few run away or things like that.
It was planned for the whole camp.
So there was not-- although there was secretive, but it was not to leave somebody normally, leave somebody.
That was planned the whole camp should go.
And the plan was we usually worked till 5 o'clock in the evening.
And that time is it already starts to get dark.
It was in October.
So there was the plan was, an hour before we stopped working, the uprising will begin.
No, the uprising was made up of in each group, wherever we worked, there was about two, three Germans would supervise or chaperoned us, supervised us.
And in each group was assigned two, three people to do-- kill other these Germans in the group with the work and do it in a so way, so hidden that nobody knew what's going on.
So, for example, there-- as I said, we had the tailors and the shoemaker and the goldsmith.
In this group, these Germans used to go in constantly because always had something making for them.
All the shoes or a-- a suit or some jewelry, things like that.
So they made appointments that they come, it's ready for them.
So they lured them into this place.
And they were always behind a curtain, always with somebody with an ax or with a hammer or whatever.
And when I came in to fit, they killed him.
And then the groups where I worked at, there was in the warehouse there, they called in the German, said there's something they want to ask him.
And there was somebody there and they killed him, too, and left him there.
Now there was one in the office.
It was a German.
And he had to be killed.
So there were two guys were appointed to do it.
Now one of them got scared.
They said, I don't go.
In the meantime already, 10 Germans were already killed.
So I said-- so someone said to him, you have to go.
We are in no situation.
It's no walk back.
No, he said, I don't go.
I-- I am afraid.
So I-- I-- at once I become a big hero.
I say, we have already so many killed.
There's no way back.
If we don't go through with, we're for sure dead.
So I-- Selma gave me from the warehouse a kitchen knife and I went with-- together with the other fellow.
I went in and we killed the German.
And I jabbed him with the knife.
And I say, this is for my father, for my brother, for all the Jewish which you killed.
And we killed him.
It was luck.
He was lying bleeding.
And-- and the minute we came out of this office was a truck with a German, German that came in a truck.
And I was bleeding, too.
I had a cut here and I was bleeding.
But Selma was there.
And somehow he didn't see me.
And Selma gave me quick a bandage-- ah-- a bandage-- a-- a-- a handkerchief or something like that.
And it was 5 o'clock.
And the group started to go back to the place where we live.
It was 5 o'clock.
And I joined the group, and I went back to the place where we were live.
When we came there, there was different groups from all the places came, and it was a big confusion, a big-- everybody was-- you came from someplace what happened.
Everybody knew already what was going on.
So, and there was some Ukrainian came, and we killed him there on the spot, too, because-- and there was [? going around ?] in the camp, [? going around ?] there was an hour was going.
The electricity was cut off.
The telephone was cut off because they're afraid maybe somebody would come and communicate outside.
So-- so when we came back, there was 5 o'clock, and we all start to running in all directions.
A lot of people run in the mines, got killed there.
A lot of people went to the front gate and got killed there, too.
And there was-- there was some shooting because some of the Jews had an access to the warehouse from ammunition.
So they got some guns and some guns and revolvers and things like that.
So there was a lot of shooting going on.
So the Germans start some-- whoever is still left was afraid, I guess.
So I see still Frenzel.
He had a-- a bucket in the front of his, and he start to shoot.
But there were so people start to running.
So I grabbed Selma.
I said, let's run on the front-- front gate.
And we ran to the front gate.
A lot of people fell dead here.
It was not another way.
We had to do, because if you don't run, you're dead.
If you run, have a chance.
But we had.
And we ran.
We had to still to run about a quarter mile till the woods.
It was raining, and she had some boots on.
And somehow it was not good there.
She had to took it off.
So there was half in the boot and half boot in the foot.
And we run in the woods.
We still run in the woods.
So we think, oh, we are in the woods.
It's better.
And then we ran in the woods.
Yeah.
I knew.
Well, I took some clothes because we had access to the clothes.
And the valuables what I always sent to the men there, I put it on on my legs, different valuable things, rings, gold, diamonds, whatever, money.
And I bandage this around, because if they caught on you something, you are dead.
So I figured the last day I have to take the chance.
So that was the way I took with me valuable things, what helped out later.
So-- so we ran in the group, we ran in the woods.
And then we found another group of older inmates which ran away.
